Threat.value: Taking too many risks or doing things that could cause you harm
Suddenly feeling or acting as if the stressful experience were actually happening again

Memory.value: My memories of the frightening event are like a film that plays over and over.
When I think about the frightening event I can sometimes smell things that I smelt when the frightening event happened.
I remember the frightening event as a few moments, and each moment is a picture in my mind.

Negative Appraisals.value: Somebody else would have stopped the event from happening
Nothing good can happen to me anymore

Maladaptive Strategies.value: I detach myself from the memories

vignette:
He is a 59-year-old South Asian married man with a military trauma history who presents with persistent post-traumatic stress symptoms, with a PCL-5 score of 36. In day-to-day life he is particularly unsettled by emergency contexts, especially ambulances, and by unpredictable situations or certain physical locations that seem to carry the emotional weight of the past. These moments quickly pull him into a heightened sense of danger, and he finds himself on guard, concentrating less well and sometimes taking risks or doing things that could place him in harm’s way. At times he describes suddenly feeling or acting as if the traumatic event were happening again, which leaves him visibly shaken and difficult to settle.

His memories of the frightening event are vivid but fragmentary, experienced as a film that plays over and over, with a series of snapshot-like moments rather than a coherent narrative. He also reports that when he thinks about it, he can sometimes smell the same odours present at the time of the trauma. These sensory fragments intrude unexpectedly and are linked with a stronger sense that danger is still near. They also draw him back toward reminders of the event, so that ordinary cues in the present are repeatedly pulled into the orbit of the past.

His view of himself and the future is notably bleak. He feels that somebody else would have stopped the event from happening, that nothing good can happen to him anymore, and that he cannot rely on himself. These beliefs sit heavily with him and appear to shape the way he meets reminders of the trauma. He tends to think of something else, numb his feelings, or detach from the memories. Rather than bringing relief, these strategies often leave the memories intact and the sense of threat unresolved.

Over time, the combination of intrusive memory, heightened alertness, and repeated exposure to triggers has taken a toll on his daily functioning and relationships. He appears trapped in a cycle in which reminders, mental images, and self-critical appraisals keep the trauma psychologically close. His coping is largely focused on getting through the moment, but the cost is emotional distance, reduced concentration, and a narrowing of his life around the possibility of threat.
